sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 4

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 4:1
items.html:
After the restriction and the screen that was placed over the desire to receive, it was disqualified from being a vessel of reception and departed from the system of Sanctity; in its place the Returning Light serves as the vessel of reception, and the vessel of the desire to receive was given over to the system of impurity.
After the restriction and the screen that was placed over this vessel, called the desire to receive, it was annulled and separated and departed from the whole system of Sanctity, and in its place the Returning Light was established as the vessel of reception (as written in Part Three). And know that this is the entire difference between ABYA of Sanctity and ABYA of impurity: the vessels of reception of ABYA of Sanctity are of the Returning Light, corrected in equivalence of form with Ein Sof, while ABYA of impurity make use of the restricted desire to receive, which is a form opposite to Ein Sof. Thereby they are severed and separated from the Life of Lives, which is Ein Sof.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 4:2
items.html:
Man is nourished by the dregs of the Klipot, and therefore uses the desire to receive as they do.
By this you will understand the root of the corruptions, which was included at once in the thought of creation — which is to delight His creatures. After the whole cascading of the five comprehensive worlds — Adam Kadmon and ABYA — the Klipot [shells] were revealed as well, in the four worlds ABYA of impurity, in the secret of "God has made them one opposite the other." Then we find before us the turbid, corporeal body, of which it is said, "the inclination of a man's heart is evil from his youth," because from his youth all his nourishment is from the dregs of the Klipot. The whole matter of the Klipot and impurity is the form of the "desire only to receive" that is in them, having nothing whatsoever of the desire to bestow. In this they stand opposite to Him, for He has no desire to receive at all, God forbid, and His entire desire is only to delight and to bestow. Therefore the Klipot are called "dead," for being opposite in form to the Life of Lives, they are severed from Him and have nothing of His abundance. Hence the body, too, nourished by the dregs of the Klipot, is severed from life and is full of foulness. And all this is because of the "desire only to receive" and not to bestow that is imprinted in it — its desire is always open to take the world and all its fullness into its belly. Therefore "the wicked, in their lives, are called dead," for through their disparity of form to the very extreme from their root, having nothing of bestowal, they are severed from Him and are literally dead. And though it seems that the wicked, too, have something of bestowal — giving charity and the like — it has already been said of them in the Zohar that "every grace they do, they do for themselves," their chief intention being for themselves and their own honor. But the righteous, who engage in Torah and Mitzvot not in order to receive a prize but to bestow contentment upon their Maker, purify their bodies thereby and invert their vessels of reception toward bestowal — as our holy rabbi said: "It is revealed and known… that I have not enjoyed even with my little finger" (Ketubot 104). By this they literally adhere to Him, their form being entirely equivalent to their Maker's, with no disparity of form at all. And this is what our sages expounded on the verse "and say to Zion: you are My people" — as expounded in the Introduction to the Zohar, item 67: "you are My people [Ami]" — "with Me [Imi], in partnership": the righteous are partners with the Creator, for He began the creation, and the righteous complete it by inverting the vessels of reception into vessels of bestowal.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 4:3
items.html:
The whole of reality is included in Ein Sof and extends as existence from existence; only the desire to receive is new, and extends as existence from absence.
Know that the entire novelty that the Creator brought forth in this creation — of which our sages said that He brought it forth as existence from absence — falls on nothing but the form of the desire to enjoy imprinted in every creature. Beyond that, nothing new occurred in the secret of creation. This is the secret of "who forms light and creates darkness": the Ramban explained that the word "creates" indicates a novelty — something that had not existed before. You see that it is not written "and creates light," for there is no novelty in the light by way of existence from absence: the Light, and all that is included in the Light — all the pleasant sensations and conceptions in the world — extend as existence from existence, that is, they are already included in Him; hence there is no novelty in them, and so it says "forms light," indicating that it contains no novelty and no creation. But of the darkness, which includes every unpleasant sensation and conception, it says "and creates darkness," for He brought them forth literally as existence from absence — that is, this is not, God forbid, in His own reality at all, but was newly made now. And the root of them all is the form of the "desire to enjoy" included in His Lights that expand from Him: at first it is merely dimmer than the upper Light, and is therefore called darkness relative to the Light; but in the end the Klipot, the Sitra Achra [the other side], and the wicked cascade and come forth by reason of it — those who are entirely severed through it from the root of life. This is the secret of the verse "her feet go down to death": "her feet" indicates the end of a matter, saying that the feet of Malchut — the desire to enjoy present in the expansion of His Light — cascade at their end into death for the Sitra Achra, and for those who are nourished by the Sitra Achra and drawn after it.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 4:4
items.html:
Being branches extending from Ein Sof, the things found in our root are a pleasure to us, and those not in our root are a burden and a torment to us.
Yet one may ask: since this disparity of form of the desire to receive must necessarily exist in the creatures — for otherwise, how would they extend from Him and pass from being Creator to being creature, which is conceivable only through disparity of form, as said above — and moreover, this form of the desire to enjoy is the principal goodness of the whole creation, around which the thought of creation revolves, and it is also the measure of the quantity of the goodness and delight, as we elaborated above, wherefore it is called "place" — how then can it be said of it that it is called darkness, and that it extends down to death, making in the lower receivers a cessation and separation, God forbid, from the Life of Lives? And we must also understand this great dread that comes upon the receivers because of the disparity of form from His Essence — why all this great anger?
To explain this subtle matter to you sufficiently, we must first clarify the origin of the pleasures and torments felt in our world. Perceive this: it is known that every branch is of the same nature as its root; therefore, everything practiced in the root, the branch too will desire, love, and covet; and everything not practiced in the root, the branch too withdraws from, cannot tolerate, and hates. This law holds between every root and its branch, and shall not pass. Now, since He is the root of all the creatures He created, everything included in Him, extending to us from Him directly, is sweet and pleasant to us, for our nature is close to our root; and everything not found in Him, extending to us not from Him directly but by the pivot of the creation itself, goes against our nature and is hard for us to bear. Thus: we love rest, and we hate motion so much that we make no movement except to attain rest — because our root is not one of motion but of rest; motion is not practiced in it at all, and therefore motion is against our nature and hateful to us. Likewise we love wisdom, might, wealth, and all the virtues — because they are included in Him, who is our root; and we hate their opposites, such as foolishness, weakness, poverty, and disgrace — because they are not found in our root at all, which makes them loathsome and hateful to us beyond endurance.
Yet we must inquire: how can any extension come to us that is not directly from Him, but from the pivot of the creation itself? This is like a rich man who called a man from the market and fed him and gave him drink, and granted him silver and gold every single day, each day more than the day before. Observe that this man tastes two flavors at once in the rich man's enormous gifts, each unlike the other: on the one hand he tastes boundless pleasure from the abundance of the gifts; on the other, it is hard for him to bear the abundance of the benefaction, and he is ashamed at receiving it — the very abundance heaped upon him each time breeds in him an impatience. It is certain that the pleasure he has from the gifts extends to him directly from the rich giver; but the hardship of bearing it, which he tastes in the gifts, does not extend to him from the rich giver, but from the receiver's own essence — shame awakens in him because of the receiving, the free gift. In truth the rich man causes this too, of course, but in an indirect way.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 4:5
items.html:
Because the desire to receive is not found in our root, we feel shame and impatience in it. This is what our sages said — that to correct it, toil in Torah and Mitzvot was "prepared" for us in this world, to invert the desire to receive into a desire to bestow.
What emerges from all that has been said is that all the forms that extend to us indirectly from Him carry in them a hardship of endurance, being against our nature. By this you will understand that the new form made in the receiver — the "desire to enjoy" — is truly no lowliness or deficiency with respect to Him; on the contrary, it is the very pivot of His creation, without which there is no creation at all, as above. It is the receiver, the bearer of that form, who feels in it, of his own account, the hardship of endurance — because that form is not found in his root. Study this well.
Thus we have succeeded in understanding the answer of our sages, that this world was created because "one who eats of that which is not his own fears to look upon his own face," which at first seemed so perplexing, as above. Now their words become sweet to us: their intention is upon the disparity of form of the "desire to enjoy" that is necessarily in the souls, as above. Because "one who eats of that which is not his own fears to look upon his own face" — every receiver of a gift is ashamed at receiving it, by reason of the disparity of form from the root, in which this form of reception does not exist. To correct this, He created this world, into which the soul comes and clothes herself in a body; and through engagement in Torah and Mitzvot in order to bring contentment to her Maker, the soul's vessels of reception are inverted into vessels of bestowal. That is: of herself she would not have desired the honored abundance, but she receives the abundance in order to bestow contentment upon her Maker, who wishes the souls to enjoy His abundance. And since she is clean of the desire to receive for herself, she is no longer afraid to look upon her own face — and thereby the full perfection of the creature is revealed. The matter of the need and the necessity for the cascading so far down as this world will be clarified below: the great work mentioned above — the inversion of the form of reception into the form of bestowal — is conceivable only in this world, as will yet be written.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 4:6
items.html:
The wicked — their breaking is a double breaking; and the righteous inherit double.
Come and see that for the wicked, their breaking is a double breaking, for they hold the rope at both ends: this world was created with lack and emptiness of all the good abundance, and to acquire possessions one needs motion. Yet it is known that an abundance of motion pains a person, being an extension that is not direct from His essence. But to remain empty of possessions and of good is also impossible, for this too is opposed to the root, since the root is full of every good. Therefore they choose the suffering of much motion in order to attain the fullness of possessions. But since all their possessions and property are for themselves alone — and "he who has a hundred wants two hundred" — "no man dies with half his desire in his hand." So they suffer from both sides: from the pain of the abundance of motion, and from the pain of the lack of possessions, half of which they are missing. But the righteous "shall inherit double in their land": after they invert their "desire to receive" into a desire to bestow, and what they receive is in order to bestow, they inherit double — for besides attaining the perfection of the delight and the choicest of possessions, they also attain equivalence of form with their Maker, whereby they stand in true adhesion. And then they stand also in the secret of rest, for the abundance comes to them of itself, without any motion or toil.
